epic.name: Schedule & Rotation Data Model + API
epic.objective: Define the core data models for on-call schedules, rotation layers, and overrides. Implement full CRUD API and the schedule evaluation logic (who is on call at a given time).
epic.definition_of_done: Users can create schedules with rotation layers and overrides via the REST API. The /oncall endpoint returns the correct on-call user for any given timestamp.

tasks.name: Design schedule and rotation data models
tasks.description: Create database migrations and Go models for Schedule, ScheduleLayer, ScheduleOverride, and ScheduleParticipant
acceptance_criteria: Schedule model: id, name, description, timezone (IANA string), created_at, updated_at
ScheduleLayer model: id, schedule_id, name, order_index, rotation_type (daily/weekly/custom), rotation_start (timestamp), shift_duration_seconds, created_at
ScheduleParticipant model: id, layer_id, user_id (or user_name for v0.4 simplicity), order_index
ScheduleOverride model: id, schedule_id, start_time, end_time, override_user_id (or user_name), created_by, created_at
Database migrations (000010_create_schedules, 000011_create_schedule_layers, 000012_create_schedule_overrides)
ScheduleRepository: CRUD for schedules, layers, overrides, participants
All timestamps stored in UTC; timezone only used for display
tasks.implementation_notes: Keep user references as plain strings (user_name / slack_user_id) for v0.4 — no User model yet. A User model with auth comes in enterprise tier. shift_duration_seconds allows sub-day rotations for future flexibility.

tasks.name: Implement schedule evaluation engine
tasks.description: Build the logic to compute who is on call for a given schedule at any point in time
acceptance_criteria: ScheduleEvaluator.WhoIsOnCall(scheduleID, at time.Time) (string, error) returns on-call user
Evaluation order: check overrides first (exact time window match), then base rotation layers by order_index
Layer evaluation: given rotation_start, shift_duration_seconds, and participants list, compute the current participant via modulo arithmetic
Returns empty string (no one on call) if schedule has no layers/participants
Unit tests: daily rotation, weekly rotation, override shadows base rotation, multiple layers (first non-empty wins)
Handles edge cases: participant list empty, shift_duration zero, timezone conversion
tasks.implementation_notes: The core algorithm: elapsed = at - rotation_start; shift_index = (elapsed / shift_duration) % len(participants); on_call = participants[shift_index]. Override check: if any override covers `at`, return override_user. No caching needed — evaluation is O(layers × participants) and fast.

tasks.name: Schedules CRUD API
tasks.description: REST API endpoints for managing schedules, layers, participants, and overrides
acceptance_criteria: GET /api/v1/schedules → list all schedules
GET /api/v1/schedules/:id → get schedule with layers and participants
POST /api/v1/schedules → create schedule
PATCH /api/v1/schedules/:id → update schedule metadata
DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id → delete schedule (cascades layers/overrides)
POST /api/v1/schedules/:id/layers → add rotation layer
DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id/layers/:layer_id → remove layer
GET /api/v1/schedules/:id/oncall → who is on call now (optional ?at= timestamp)
GET /api/v1/schedules/:id/oncall/timeline → on-call timeline for a date range (?from=&to=)
POST /api/v1/schedules/:id/overrides → create override
DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id/overrides/:override_id → delete override
Validation: timezone must be valid IANA zone, end_time > start_time for overrides
tasks.implementation_notes: The /oncall/timeline endpoint is used by the calendar UI to render upcoming shifts. It should return an array of {start, end, user} segments for the requested window. Keep pagination simple — max 30 days per request.
